statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find all elements that appear more than n/3 times. The array will have at least one such element. The problem is a variation of the classic majority element problem, but here we need to find all elements that appear more than n/3 times, not just one. The solution should be efficient and scalable. The Boyer-Moore Majority Vote algorithm can be used to solve this problem. The algorithm works by essentially maintaining a counter for the majority element. If the counter is zero, we set the current element as the majority element. Then, for each element, if it is the same as the majority element, we increment the counter; otherwise, we decrement the counter. At the end, we need to check if the majority element really appears more than n/3 times. We can do this by iterating through the array and counting the occurrences of the majority element. If the count is greater than n/3, we add the majority element to the result list. We can use this algorithm twice to find all elements that appear more than n/3 times.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a list of integers, the elements that appear more than n/3 times in the array.
statement.notes: The input array will have at least one element that appears more than n/3 times. The output list should be in the same order as the elements appear in the input array.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The element 3 appears 3 times, which is more than n/3 = 2 times.
The elements 1 and 2 both appear more than n/3 = 2 times.

hints.text: To solve this problem, we need to find all elements that appear more than n/3 times in the array. A good starting point would be to understand the Boyer-Moore Majority Vote algorithm, which is used to find the majority element in an array.

The Boyer-Moore Majority Vote algorithm works by maintaining a counter for the majority element. If the counter is zero, we set the current element as the majority element. Then, for each element, if it is the same as the majority element, we increment the counter; otherwise, we decrement the counter. This process helps us to find the majority element.

Since we need to find all elements that appear more than n/3 times, we can use the Boyer-Moore Majority Vote algorithm twice. In the first pass, we find the first candidate for the majority element. In the second pass, we find the second candidate for the majority element. After that, we need to verify if these candidates really appear more than n/3 times by counting their occurrences in the array.

editorial.title: Majority Element II – From Brute Force to Boyer–Moore
editorial.intuition: The problem asks for all numbers that appear more than ⌊n/3⌋ times. In any array of size n there can be at most two such numbers, because three distinct numbers each exceeding n/3 would already exceed n elements. This simple pigeonhole fact is the key that lets us reduce the search space dramatically.
editorial.bruteForce: A straightforward solution counts every element’s frequency, for example with a hash map or by sorting and scanning. This guarantees correctness but costs O(n) time and O(n) space, or O(n log n) time if sorting is used. For the given limits (n up to 10 000) it runs fast enough, yet it is still linear in both time and auxiliary memory, which is unnecessary for this problem.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is the Boyer–Moore majority vote algorithm extended to two candidates. It keeps at most two potential majority elements and their counters while scanning the array once. Whenever a new element differs from both candidates and both counters are non‑zero, both counters are decremented. When a counter reaches zero, the current element becomes the new candidate for that slot. After the first pass we have at most two candidates; a second pass verifies whether they truly exceed the ⌊n/3⌋ threshold.
editorial.optimalSolution: The algorithm runs in O(n) time and uses only O(1) extra space. It first identifies up to two candidates that could be majority elements, then confirms them by counting occurrences. The final result is sorted before output.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Let S be the set of all elements appearing more than n/3 times. By the pigeonhole principle |S|≤2. During the first pass, whenever an element not in S is encountered, it can only reduce the counters of existing candidates or replace a zero counter. Because elements of S occur frequently, they will never be fully eliminated; at the end, any element of S must be one of the two stored candidates. The second pass counts actual frequencies, ensuring that only true majority elements are output. Thus the algorithm returns exactly S.
editorial.dryRun: Consider nums=[1,2,3,1,2,1,1]. n=7, threshold=2. Pass 1: start cand1=1,cnt1=1; x=2→cand2=2,cnt2=1; x=3→cnt1=0,cnt2=0; x=1→cand1=1,cnt1=1; x=2→cand2=2,cnt2=1; x=1→cnt1=2; x=1→cnt1=3. Candidates: 1 and 2. Pass 2: count(1)=4>2, count(2)=2≤2. Result=[1].
commonMistakes: Assuming more than two majority elements can exist; forgetting the pigeonhole bound.
Using the first pass results directly without a verification pass, leading to false positives when no element exceeds the threshold.
Implementing the decrement step incorrectly, e.g., decrementing only one counter instead of both.
Not resetting counters when they reach zero, causing stale candidates to persist.
Sorting the result unnecessarily after verification, which adds O(k log k) time where k≤2.
Using a hash map for counting in the verification step, which defeats the O(1) space goal.
